perturbation.original_text: 1.1 "Full Verification Service" means those bespoke tests agreed between the Licensee and NCC for the verification of the Material; 1.2 "Intellectual Property Rights" means copyright, trade secret, patent, and all other rights of a similar nature; 1.3 "Licence Agreement" means the licence granted to the Licensee for the Package; 1.4 "Material" means the source code of the Package comprising the latest technical information and documentation described in Schedules 1 and 2; 1.5 "Package" means the software package licensed to the Licensee under the Licence Agreement; and 1.6 "Standard Verification Service" means those tests detailed in the Standard Verification Service published by NCC from time to time.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 "Full Verification Service" means verification tests determined by NCC; 1.2 "Intellectual Property Rights" means rights of a similar nature; 1.3 "Licence Agreement" means the agreement granted to the Licensee for the Package; 1.4 "Material" means the documentation described in Schedules 1; 1.5 "Package" means the software package licensed to the Licensee under the Licence Agreement; and 1.6 "Standard Verification Service" means verification tests performed by NCC.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the definitions in Section 1 introduces uncertainty and contradictions. Specifically, the definition of 'Full Verification Service' is changed to mean tests determined by NCC, removing the requirement for agreement with the Licensee. The definition of 'Intellectual Property Rights' is shortened, omitting 'copyright, trade secret, patent'. The 'Material' definition now only references schedule 1, instead of 'Schedules 1 and 2' as it was initially. This change creates an in-text contradiction because Schedule 2 refers to information required within the material that should be supplied. This contradicts section 2.1.5 that expects information detailed in schedule 2, to be delivered with each deposit of material, but because Schedule 2 is no longer contemplated, creates the in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1.5 deliver with each deposit of the Material the information detailed in Schedule 2.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of 'Material' to only reference Schedule 1, whereas the original definition included Schedules 1 and 2. Section 2.1.5 explicitly requires delivery of information detailed in Schedule 2 with each deposit of the Material. By excluding Schedule 2 from the definition of 'Material', the changed text directly contradicts section 2.1.5, making it unclear what information needs to be delivered, and potentially unenforcable.

perturbation.original_text: 6.2.3 a signed confidentiality undertaking as detailed in Schedule 3 then NCC will release the Material to the Licensee upon receipt of the release fee stated in Schedule 4.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.2.3 then NCC will release the Material to the Licensee upon receipt of the release fee stated in Schedule 4.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement of 'a signed confidentiality undertaking as detailed in Schedule 3', before releasing the material, creates an in-text contradiction, since Schedule 3 is the specific 'Confidentiality Undertaking' which creates uncertainty, since section 7.1, indicates that the material should remain confidential property, but since there is no confidential agreement that needs to be signed, it creates an in-text contradiction with section 7.1
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7.1 The Material shall remain the confidential property of the Owner and in the event that NCC provides a copy of the Material to the Licensee, the Licensee shall be permitted to use the Material only in accordance with a confidentiality undertaking in the form contained in Schedule 3.
perturbation.justification: The original text in 6.2.3 required 'a signed confidentiality undertaking as detailed in Schedule 3' before releasing material. The changed text removes this requirement. This directly contradicts section 7.1, which states that the 'Licensee shall be permitted to use the Material only in accordance with a confidentiality undertaking in the form contained in Schedule 3.' By removing the requirement to sign the undertaking, the licensee can now access and potentially use the material without the necessary confidentiality agreement, directly violating the terms outlined in section 7.1.

perturbation.original_text: 11.4 If the Licence Agreement has expired or has been lawfully terminated this Agreement will automatically terminate on the same date.
perturbation.changed_text: 11.4 This Agreement will automatically terminate on the same date.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the condition 'If the Licence Agreement has expired or has been lawfully terminated' creates an in-text contradiction with the rest of the conditions in section 11, which refer to different ways that the agreement can be terminated. By removing the condition and indicating that 'This Agreement will automatically terminate on the same date.' without specifying, creates an internal contradiction since it doesn't provide the appropriate context of what date is it reffering to.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 11 Termination 11.1 NCC may terminate this Agreement after failure by the Owner or the Licensee to comply with a 30 day written notice from NCC to pay any outstanding fee. If the failure to pay is on the part of the Owner the Licensee shall be given the option of paying such fee itself. Such amount will be recoverable by the Licensee direct from the Owner. 11.2 NCC may terminate this Agreement by giving 60 days written notice to the Owner and the Licensee. In that event the Owner and the Licensee shall appoint a mutually acceptable new custodian on terms similar to those contained in this Agreement. 11.3 If a new custodian is not appointed within 30 days of delivery of any notice issued by NCC in accordance with the provisions of Clause 11.2, the Owner or the Licensee shall be entitled to request the President for the time being of the British Computer Society to appoint a suitable new custodian upon such terms and conditions as he shall require. Such appointment shall be final and binding on all parties. 11.4 If the Licence Agreement has expired or has been lawfully terminated this Agreement will automatically terminate on the same date. 11.5 The Licensee may terminate this Agreement at any time by giving written notice to the Owner and NCC. 11.6 The Owner may only terminate this Agreement with the written consent of the Licensee. 11.7 This Agreement shall terminate upon release of the Material to the Licensee in accordance with Clause 6. 11.8 Upon termination under the provisions of Clauses 11.2, 11.4, 11.5 or 11.6 NCC will deliver the Material to the Owner. If NCC is unable to trace the Owner NCC will destroy the Material. 11.9 Upon termination under the provisions of Clause 11.1 the Material will be available for collection by the Owner from NCC for 30 days from the date of termination. After such 30 day period NCC will destroy the Material. 11.10 NCC may forthwith terminate this Agreement and destroy the Material if it is unable to trace the Owner having used all reasonable endeavours to do so.
perturbation.justification: The original text states 'If the Licence Agreement has expired or has been lawfully terminated this Agreement will automatically terminate on the same date.' which provides a specific condition for termination. The changed text removes this condition, stating 'This Agreement will automatically terminate on the same date.' without specifying under what circumstances this occurs. This directly contradicts other clauses within section 11 (the 'Contradicted Text') that outline different termination scenarios based on NCC's actions, licensee's actions, or owner's actions. By removing the specific condition, the changed text creates ambiguity and inconsistency regarding termination, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction as it misaligns with the other conditions for termination in Section 11.
